failure_surface: Target cells contained ArrayFormula objects that were not properly inspected before overwrite; data range boundaries were assumed rather than dynamically determined from actual sheet metadata.
root_cause: The agent assumed fixed row/column boundaries (rows 2-42, columns 2-21) without verifying against ws.max_row, ws.max_column, and existing cell content types. Additionally, openpyxl represents certain formula types as formula objects rather than simple strings, which may not handle correctly during iteration and overwrite operations without explicit type checking.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: Before writing to any target range, first inspect the cell content types (value, formula string, formula object), verify sheet dimensions using ws.max_row/max_column, and confirm data boundaries match expected patterns before applying transformations.
evidence: Initial output shows ArrayFormula objects present from Row 26-43 across all 7 columns, indicating pre-existing formula types not accounted for
Agent assumed data_rows_end=42 based on unique drivers count, but ws.max_row returned 118, suggesting boundary assumption may be incorrect
Column header inspection revealed 'None' values in columns (1, 22, 23, 24, 25), requiring validation logic beyond simple truthiness checks
Code wrote formulas directly via cell_ref.value = formula without checking if cell contained formula objects that require special handling

memory_items.title: Verify Cell Content Types Before Overwrite
memory_items.lesson: When iterating to overwrite cells, check both cell.value and cell.data_type to determine if cells contain formula objects, formula strings, or plain values before applying changes.
memory_items.generalization: In spreadsheet manipulation tasks, always inspect target cell content types using hasattr(cell, 'formula') and type(cell.value) checks before overwriting to prevent losing or corrupting existing formula structures.

memory_items.title: Dynamically Determine Data Boundaries
memory_items.lesson: Never hardcode row/column boundaries based on visual inspection or assumptions; use ws.max_row, ws.max_column, and programmatic detection of last non-empty cells.
memory_items.generalization: For robust spreadsheet processing, calculate data ranges from actual sheet properties (max_row, max_column) rather than assuming boundaries based on task description or sample rows.

memory_items.title: Handle Formula Objects Explicitly
memory_items.lesson: Some formula types (like ArrayFormula in Google Sheets converted to xlsx) appear as formula objects in openpyxl, requiring special handling beyond standard value assignment.
memory_items.generalization: When working with openpyxl, account for formula object types that may not serialize correctly by explicitly converting to formula strings or removing existing formula attributes before reassignment.

edits.content:
## 6. Cell Content Inspection
- **Type Checking**: Before overwriting any cell, check if it contains formula objects using hasattr(cell, 'formula'), and verify cell.data_type to distinguish between values, formulas, and shared strings.
- **Boundary Verification**: Always use ws.max_row and ws.max_column to confirm data range limits rather than assuming boundaries from task descriptions or partial inspections.
- **Formula Objects**: Be aware that some formula types (especially ArrayFormula) may appear as formula objects in openpyxl; convert these to formula strings explicitly before saving to ensure proper serialization.
